cheatSheet:
# Apache Kafka — Quick Reference

## Core Concepts

### Topics
- Named stream of records (like a database table, but append-only)
- Durable, ordered log; consumers can re-read at any time
- Retention controlled by time (`retention.ms`) or size (`retention.bytes`)

### Partitions
- A topic is split into N partitions for parallelism
- Each partition is an ordered, immutable sequence with per-message **offsets** starting at 0
- More partitions = more throughput; cannot reduce partition count without recreating topic
- Unit of parallelism: max concurrent consumers in a group = number of partitions

### Brokers
- Individual Kafka server nodes; identified by integer broker ID
- One broker per cluster is elected **Controller** (manages leader elections, topic metadata)
- Each broker stores partition replicas and serves producer/consumer requests
- Horizontal scaling: add brokers, reassign partitions

### Consumer Groups
- Set of consumers sharing the same `group.id`
- Kafka assigns each partition to exactly one consumer within the group
- Multiple groups consume the same topic independently (broadcast pattern)
- Offsets tracked per group per partition in `__consumer_offsets` topic

### Producers
- Publish `ProducerRecord(topic, key?, value)` to a topic
- Partition selection: hash(key) % partitions | round-robin (no key) | custom Partitioner
- Buffering: `batch.size` + `linger.ms` control batching for throughput
- Compression: `snappy` (CPU-balanced), `lz4` (fast), `gzip` (best ratio), `zstd`

### Consumers
- Pull-based: consumer calls `poll()` in a loop; Kafka never pushes
- `max.poll.records` limits records per poll; `max.poll.interval.ms` is the heartbeat deadline
- `auto.offset.reset`: `earliest` (read all), `latest` (only new)

### Offsets
- Integer position of a record within a partition (0-indexed)
- Committed offset = last successfully processed position for a consumer group
- Commit strategies: auto-commit, sync, async, per-partition

### Replication
- `replication.factor` copies of each partition across brokers
- One replica is **Leader** (handles all reads/writes); others are **Followers**
- RF=3 tolerates 1 broker failure; RF=5 tolerates 2

### ISR (In-Sync Replicas)
- Set of replicas caught up within `replica.lag.time.max.ms` (default 30 s)
- ISR shrinks when a follower falls behind; expands when it catches up
- Only ISR members are eligible for leader election (unless unclean leader election enabled)

### Acks
| acks | Durability | Latency |
|------|-----------|--------|
| 0 | Fire-and-forget; data loss possible | Lowest |
| 1 | Leader ack only; loss if leader crashes before replication | Medium |
| all (-1) | All ISR ack; safest | Highest |

### Exactly-Once Semantics (EOS)
- **Idempotent producer**: `enable.idempotence=true` — deduplicates retries via producer ID + sequence number
- **Transactional producer**: `transactional.id` — atomic multi-partition writes + offset commits
- **Consumer**: `isolation.level=read_committed` — skips uncommitted/aborted records
- **Kafka Streams**: `processing.guarantee=exactly_once_v2`

### Kafka Streams
- Java library for stream processing inside the Kafka ecosystem
- DSL: `filter`, `map`, `flatMap`, `groupBy`, `aggregate`, `join`
- Low-level Processor API for custom stateful logic
- State stored in local RocksDB, backed up to changelog topics
- Exactly-once via `processing.guarantee=exactly_once_v2`

### Kafka Connect
- Framework for integrating Kafka with external systems without writing producers/consumers
- **Source Connector**: External system → Kafka (e.g., MySQL CDC, S3)
- **Sink Connector**: Kafka → External system (e.g., Elasticsearch, BigQuery)
- Runs in distributed mode (cluster) or standalone mode
- Schema Registry + Avro/Protobuf for schema evolution

## Key Config Cheat Sheet
```
Producer:
  acks=all, retries=Integer.MAX_VALUE, enable.idempotence=true
  batch.size=16384, linger.ms=5, compression.type=snappy
  max.in.flight.requests.per.connection=5 (idempotent) / 1 (strict order)

Consumer:
  enable.auto.commit=false, max.poll.records=500
  session.timeout.ms=45000, heartbeat.interval.ms=3000
  isolation.level=read_committed (EOS)

Broker:
  min.insync.replicas=2 (with RF=3)
  unclean.leader.election.enable=false (production)
  log.retention.hours=168 (7 days default)
```

resources.title: Kafka: The Definitive Guide (2nd Edition)
resources.author: Gwen Shapira, Todd Palino, Rajini Sivaram, Krit Petty
resources.justification: The canonical reference written by Confluent engineers — covers architecture, configuration, and production operations in depth.

resources.title: Apache Kafka Documentation (Official)
resources.author: Apache Software Foundation
resources.justification: Primary source for configuration parameters, APIs, and protocol details. Essential for production tuning.

resources.title: Confluent Developer — Kafka Tutorials
resources.author: Confluent
resources.justification: Hands-on tutorials with real code, Kafka Streams exercises, and schema registry — free and current.

resources.title: Designing Data-Intensive Applications
resources.author: Martin Kleppmann
resources.justification: Chapter 11 covers stream processing and Kafka in the context of distributed systems — indispensable for senior interviews.

levels.name: Novice
levels.description: Understands what Kafka is and why it exists. Can explain topics, producers, and consumers at a conceptual level.
observableSkills: Explain what a topic, partition, and offset are
Describe the producer-consumer model
Run a local Kafka broker and produce/consume messages via CLI

milestoneProject.title: Hello Kafka
milestoneProject.description: Start a local Kafka cluster with Docker Compose, create a topic, produce 100 messages with KafkaProducer, consume them with KafkaConsumer, and print offset + value.

levels.name: Advanced Beginner
levels.description: Understands replication, consumer groups, and basic producer durability settings. Can write Java producer/consumer code.
observableSkills: Configure acks=all and min.insync.replicas
Explain ISR and leader election
Implement a consumer group with manual offset commits
Explain partition key selection and its effect on ordering

milestoneProject.title: Durable Order Event Pipeline
milestoneProject.description: Build a producer that publishes order events with acks=all and a consumer group of 3 consumers that process events with manual sync commits and log partition assignment.

levels.name: Competent
levels.description: Can design multi-partition topics, implement exactly-once semantics, handle rebalancing gracefully, and tune producer/consumer configs for throughput vs. latency trade-offs.
observableSkills: Implement idempotent and transactional producers
Handle ConsumerRebalanceListener correctly
Explain unclean leader election trade-off
Choose compression codec based on workload
Monitor consumer lag

milestoneProject.title: Exactly-Once Payment Processor
milestoneProject.description: Build a transactional producer that writes payment events atomically and a consumer using isolation.level=read_committed. Simulate broker restarts and verify no duplicates.

levels.name: Proficient
levels.description: Architects Kafka-based systems for production — capacity planning, schema evolution, Kafka Streams, Connect, and cross-datacenter replication.
observableSkills: Design partition count for throughput targets
Implement a Kafka Streams topology with stateful aggregation
Configure Kafka Connect source and sink connectors
Explain MirrorMaker 2 for geo-replication
Tune JVM and OS settings for Kafka brokers

milestoneProject.title: Real-Time Analytics Pipeline
milestoneProject.description: Use Kafka Streams to compute per-user event counts over a 5-minute tumbling window, write results to a sink topic, and connect it to Elasticsearch via Kafka Connect.

levels.name: Expert
levels.description: Deep understanding of the Kafka protocol, KRaft architecture, custom interceptors, and operational excellence at scale (millions of messages/sec).
observableSkills: Explain KRaft (Raft-based metadata) vs ZooKeeper mode
Write custom Partitioner, Interceptor, and Authorizer
Diagnose ISR thrashing, consumer lag spikes, and leader skew
Design multi-tenant Kafka with quotas and ACLs
Contribute to or extend Kafka Connect connectors

milestoneProject.title: Multi-Tenant Kafka Platform
milestoneProject.description: Configure a KRaft cluster with per-tenant quotas, ACLs, schema registry, and a custom interceptor that adds trace IDs to all producer records for distributed tracing.

plan.overview: Master Apache Kafka for software engineering interviews.
plan.skippedTopics: Advanced vendor-specific features rarely asked in interviews.

sessions.title: Kafka Architecture — Brokers, Topics, Partitions, Offsets
sessions.content:
## Session 1: Kafka Architecture ```mermaid graph TB subgraph Kafka Cluster B1[Broker 1
Controller] B2[Broker 2] B3[Broker 3] end subgraph Topic: orders - 3 Partitions P0[Partition 0
Leader: B1
Replica: B2] P1[Partition 1
Leader: B2
Replica: B3] P2[Partition 2
Leader: B3
Replica: B1] end B1 --> P0 B2 --> P1 B3 --> P2 ZK[ZooKeeper / KRaft] -.->|metadata| B1 ZK -.-> B2 ZK -.-> B3 ``` Apache Kafka is a distributed, fault-tolerant, high-throughput event streaming platform. Before writing a single line of producer or consumer code, you must internalize the physical and logical layout of a Kafka cluster — because every configuration decision flows from this mental model. ### The Cluster A Kafka **cluster** is a group of **brokers** — individual server processes, each with a unique integer ID. One broker is elected the **Controller**, responsible for administrative duties: partition leader elections, topic creation and deletion, and cluster metadata propagation. If the Controller broker dies, another is automatically elected. ``` ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Kafka Cluster │ │ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌────────┐ │ │ │ Broker 1 │ │ Broker 2 │ │Broker 3│ │ │ │(Control- │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ ler) │ │ │ │ │ │ │ └──────────┘ └──────────┘ └────────┘ │ └─────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` ### Topics and Partitions A **topic** is a named, durable, ordered log of records — conceptually similar to a database table, but append-only and replayable. Every topic is divided into one or more **partitions**. A partition is the fundamental unit of parallelism and ordering in Kafka. Each record in a partition receives a monotonically increasing integer called an **offset**, starting at 0. Offsets uniquely identify a record within a partition. Consumers track their position by storing the last processed offset. ``` Topic: orders (3 partitions) Partition 0: [offset 0][offset 1][offset 2][offset 3]... Partition 1: [offset 0][offset 1][offset 2]... Partition 2: [offset 0][offset 1][offset 2][offset 3][offset 4]... ``` Kafka guarantees **ordering within a partition** but not across partitions. This is a critical design constraint: if you need all events for a given entity (e.g., a user) to be processed in order, all those events must land in the same partition. ### Replication Each partition has a configurable **replication factor** (typically 3). One replica is the **Leader** — it handles all producer writes and consumer reads. The other replicas are **Followers** — they replicate data from the leader asynchronously. The set of followers that are fully caught up is called the **ISR (In-Sync Replicas)**. ```java // Creating a topic programmatically (AdminClient) import org.apache.kafka.clients.admin.AdminClient; import org.apache.kafka.clients.admin.NewTopic; import java.util.Collections; import java.util.Properties; properties props = new Properties(); props.put("bootstrap.servers", "localhost:9092"); try (AdminClient admin = AdminClient.create(props)) { // topic name, numPartitions, replicationFactor NewTopic ordersTopic = new NewTopic("orders", 6, (short) 3); admin.createTopics(Collections.singleton(ordersTopic)).all().get(); System.out.println("Topic created: orders with 6 partitions, RF=3"); } ``` ### ZooKeeper vs KRaft Historically, Kafka depended on **Apache ZooKeeper** for cluster coordination — storing broker registrations, topic metadata, and controller election state. ZooKeeper is a separate distributed system with its own operational overhead. Kafka 2.8+ introduced **KRaft** (Kafka Raft) — a built-in consensus mechanism that eliminates the ZooKeeper dependency entirely. In KRaft mode, a quorum of broker nodes use the Raft protocol to manage cluster metadata directly. As of Kafka 3.3, KRaft is production-ready, and ZooKeeper mode is deprecated. **Key KRaft benefits:** - Simpler operations (one system instead of two) - Faster controller failover (milliseconds vs. seconds) - Supports larger clusters (millions of partitions) - Unified security model ### Storage Layout Each partition is stored as a directory of **segment files** on the broker's disk. A segment is a `.log` file (binary data) paired with an `.index` file (sparse offset index for fast seeks) and a `.timeindex` file (timestamp index). When a segment reaches `log.segment.bytes` (default 1 GB) or `log.roll.hours`, a new segment is opened. Old segments are deleted or compacted based on the retention policy. ### Interview Insight When asked "walk me through what happens when a producer sends a message to Kafka," trace the full path: producer serializes the record → determines partition → sends to partition leader's broker → leader writes to local log → leader replicates to followers → once ISR acknowledges (based on acks setting) → leader sends acknowledgment back to producer.
objectives: Explain the role of brokers, topics, partitions, and offsets in a Kafka cluster
Describe how replication and ISR provide fault tolerance
Contrast ZooKeeper-based coordination with KRaft mode
Trace the lifecycle of a message from producer to durable storage

activities.description: Start a 3-broker Kafka cluster with Docker Compose, create a topic with 6 partitions and RF=3, then use kafka-topics.sh --describe to observe partition leader and replica assignment across brokers.
Use kafka-dump-log.sh to inspect a partition's segment files and observe offset, timestamp, and key-value structure of raw records.

reviewQuestions: What is the difference between a topic and a partition?:::A topic is a logical named stream (like a table name). A partition is the physical, ordered, immutable sub-log that a topic is divided into. A topic with 6 partitions has 6 separate ordered logs, each on potentially different brokers. Producers write to partitions; consumers read from partitions. The topic is the unit of subscription; the partition is the unit of parallelism and ordering.

Why does Kafka only guarantee ordering within a partition, not across partitions?:::Partitions are independent append-only logs, potentially on different brokers, written and read concurrently. There is no global clock or sequencing across partitions. A record in partition 0 and a record in partition 1 written at the same millisecond have no ordering relationship in Kafka's data model. To enforce ordering for a logical entity, use a partition key that hashes all related records to the same partition.

What problem does KRaft solve compared to ZooKeeper?:::ZooKeeper is a separate distributed system that Kafka relied on for leader election and metadata storage — adding operational complexity, a separate failure domain, and scaling limits (ZooKeeper struggled with large numbers of partitions). KRaft embeds Raft-based consensus directly into Kafka brokers, eliminating ZooKeeper entirely. This simplifies deployment, improves controller failover speed, and removes the partition count ceiling.

If a broker crashes, how does Kafka ensure consumers can continue reading?:::Kafka replicates each partition to multiple brokers (replication.factor). When the leader broker for a partition crashes, the Kafka Controller elects a new leader from the ISR. Producers and consumers automatically discover the new leader via updated cluster metadata. Consumers re-fetch metadata and reconnect to the new leader, resuming from their last committed offset with minimal interruption.
sessions.successCriteria: Can draw the physical architecture of a Kafka cluster, explain the role of each component, and describe the path a message takes from producer to durable replicated storage.

sessions.title: Producers Deep Dive — Acks, Idempotence, Batching, Compression
objectives: Explain the internal producer pipeline from send() to broker acknowledgment
Configure acks, retries, and idempotence for production durability
Tune batch.size and linger.ms for throughput vs latency
Implement a custom Partitioner

activities.description: Write a producer that sends 10,000 messages. Measure throughput with acks=0, acks=1, and acks=all. Record latency and messages/sec for each configuration.
Simulate a network partition by stopping a follower broker mid-run. Observe whether messages are duplicated with retries alone vs. with idempotence enabled.

reviewQuestions: What is the role of batch.size vs linger.ms in producer throughput?:::batch.size is the maximum number of bytes collected for a single partition before a batch is sent. linger.ms is the maximum time the producer waits for a batch to fill before sending. Both work together: with linger.ms=0 (default), batches are sent immediately and rarely fill up; with linger.ms=10, the producer accumulates records for 10ms, producing larger, more efficient batches. Higher throughput workloads benefit most from non-zero linger.ms.

Why can retries alone cause duplicate messages, and how does idempotence fix this?:::If a producer sends a batch, the broker writes it to the log, but the ack is lost in transit (network blip), the producer times out and retries. Without idempotence, the broker has no way to detect it already wrote this batch, so it writes it again — duplicate. With idempotence, each batch carries a Producer ID + sequence number. The broker tracks the last sequence number written per producer per partition and silently discards any retry with a sequence number it has already seen.

When would you use acks=1 over acks=all?:::acks=1 is appropriate when throughput and latency are more important than guaranteed zero data loss — for example, collecting application metrics, click stream events, or IoT sensor data where occasional loss is acceptable. acks=all is required for financial transactions, order events, or any data where loss would have business consequences. The throughput difference can be 2-5x in high-volume scenarios.

What is the effect of compression.type on broker CPU usage?:::Kafka brokers do NOT decompress batches during write — they store the compressed batch as-is. Brokers only decompress when needed for operations like message format conversion or producing to older consumers. Consumers decompress batches on their side. This means compression is effectively free for brokers and benefits storage and network bandwidth. The CPU cost is paid by producers (compression) and consumers (decompression).
sessions.successCriteria: Can configure a production-grade producer with appropriate acks, idempotence, batching, and compression settings, and explain the trade-off of each parameter.

sessions.title: Consumers & Consumer Groups — Rebalancing, Offsets, Poll Loop
objectives: Explain the consumer group protocol and partition assignment
Implement the poll loop with a ConsumerRebalanceListener
Compare auto-commit, sync commit, and async commit strategies
Explain session.timeout.ms vs max.poll.interval.ms

activities.description: Run 3 consumers in the same group against a 6-partition topic. Observe partition assignment. Then kill one consumer and observe rebalance and partition redistribution.
Implement a consumer that deliberately simulates slow processing (Thread.sleep). Tune max.poll.records and max.poll.interval.ms to avoid being evicted from the group.

reviewQuestions: What is the difference between session.timeout.ms and max.poll.interval.ms?:::session.timeout.ms is the time the Group Coordinator waits for a heartbeat before declaring the consumer dead. The heartbeat thread runs in the background independently of application code. max.poll.interval.ms is the maximum time allowed between calls to poll() — if the application is stuck in a long processing loop and doesn't call poll() within this window, Kafka evicts the consumer even if heartbeats are still being sent. max.poll.interval.ms catches live-but-stuck consumers that session.timeout.ms would miss.

Why is enable.auto.commit=false recommended for production consumers?:::Auto-commit commits on a timer, not on processing completion. If the consumer polls a batch, auto-commit fires, and then the consumer crashes before finishing processing, those records are skipped — at-most-once semantics. With auto-commit disabled and manual commitSync() after processing, records are only committed after successful processing, giving at-least-once semantics. This is the correct baseline for most production consumers.

What happens to consumer lag if a consumer is stuck in processing for longer than max.poll.interval.ms?:::Kafka evicts the consumer from the group and triggers a rebalance. The stuck consumer's partitions are reassigned to other group members. Consumer lag on those partitions accumulates until the rebalance completes and another consumer resumes. The original consumer will attempt to rejoin, triggering another rebalance. To avoid this: reduce max.poll.records so each poll() batch is small enough to process within the timeout, or increase max.poll.interval.ms if processing is inherently slow.

Explain what CooperativeStickyAssignor does differently from the default assignor.:::The default assignors use 'eager' rebalancing: all consumers stop consuming, all partition assignments are revoked, and new assignments are computed from scratch — a stop-the-world pause. CooperativeStickyAssignor uses 'incremental' rebalancing: only the partitions that need to move are revoked in round one, and the rest keep consuming. In round two, the moved partitions are assigned to their new owners. This dramatically reduces the rebalance impact window for large consumer groups.
sessions.successCriteria: Can implement a production-grade consumer with manual offset commits, a rebalance listener, and correct heartbeat configuration, and explain the delivery semantic implications of each commit strategy.

sessions.title: Partitioning & Message Ordering — Keys, Custom Partitioners, Guarantees
objectives: Explain how the default partitioner routes messages with and without keys
Describe the ordering guarantee scope in Kafka
Identify and mitigate hot partition issues
Implement a custom Partitioner for a business-driven routing requirement

activities.description: Send 1000 messages with 10 distinct keys to a 6-partition topic. Print which partition each key lands on and verify the same key always maps to the same partition.
Simulate a hot partition by using a single key for 90% of messages. Monitor partition lag with kafka-consumer-groups.sh. Implement a salting strategy and observe the improvement.

reviewQuestions: Why does changing partition count break ordering for existing keys?:::Kafka uses murmur2(keyBytes) % numPartitions to route messages. When numPartitions changes, the same key hash now maps to a different partition index. Messages sent before the change for key X are in partition (hash % oldN); messages sent after are in partition (hash % newN). A consumer reading the new partition for key X will see only post-change messages, missing the history. For keys requiring strict ordering, partition count must never change, or a migration strategy (dual-writing, consumer catch-up) must be employed.

What is a hot partition and how does salting mitigate it?:::A hot partition is one that receives disproportionately more traffic than others because many messages share the same key (or no key in a sticky scenario). Salting appends a random number (e.g., 0-9) to the high-volume key before hashing, spreading writes across 10x more partitions. On the read side, consumers aggregate all salted variants. Trade-off: ordering is lost across salted sub-keys, so aggregation must be idempotent or based on timestamps rather than partition order.

When would you use a custom Partitioner instead of the default?:::Use a custom Partitioner when: (1) you have a business-driven routing requirement (e.g., route premium traffic to dedicated partitions for SLA isolation), (2) you need geographic affinity (EU events to EU-local partitions), (3) the default hash distribution is uneven for your key space, or (4) you want null-key messages to go to specific partitions rather than round-robin. Always measure actual partition distribution before and after to validate the improvement.

How do you achieve strict ordering for all events of a given entity (e.g., all order events for order-42)?:::Use order-42's ID as the partition key. The default partitioner will consistently hash this key to the same partition. All producers writing order-42 events must use the same key. The consumer must process this partition sequentially (not in parallel threads per record). If you need exactly-once + ordering, combine idempotent producer (enable.idempotence=true) with max.in.flight.requests.per.connection=5 and let the broker sequence numbers enforce ordering during retries.
sessions.successCriteria: Can explain the ordering guarantee scope of Kafka, design a partition key strategy for a given use case, identify hot partitions, and implement a custom Partitioner.

sessions.title: Replication & Fault Tolerance — ISR, Leader Election, min.insync.replicas
objectives: Explain the ISR mechanism and what causes a replica to leave and rejoin the ISR
Configure replication factor and min.insync.replicas for a given durability target
Describe the trade-off between unclean leader election and data loss
Identify and diagnose leader skew in a Kafka cluster

activities.description: Create a topic with RF=3 and min.insync.replicas=2. Stop one broker and verify producers can still write. Stop a second broker and observe NotEnoughReplicasException. Restart a broker and verify recovery.
Use kafka-topics.sh --describe to observe ISR changes as brokers are stopped and started. Record how long it takes for a follower to rejoin the ISR after reconnection.

reviewQuestions: What causes a replica to be removed from the ISR?:::A replica is removed from the ISR when it fails to fetch messages from the leader within replica.lag.time.max.ms (default 30 seconds). This can happen due to: network congestion between brokers, disk I/O saturation slowing the follower, Java GC pauses causing the follower to stall, or the follower broker being heavily loaded. The leader tracks the last fetch time for each follower and evicts any that exceed the lag threshold. The follower rejoins the ISR automatically once it catches up with the leader's log end offset.

Explain the scenario where RF=3, min.insync.replicas=2, and 2 brokers fail simultaneously.:::If 2 out of 3 brokers fail, the ISR on the surviving broker has size=1, which is below min.insync.replicas=2. With acks=all, the producer cannot get the required number of acknowledgments and receives NotEnoughReplicasException. Writes are rejected — this is the correct behavior, as acknowledging a write with only 1 replica would risk data loss if that replica also fails. Reads may still be served from the surviving broker if it holds the partition leader. Once one failed broker is restored, the ISR recovers to 2 and writes resume.

What is unclean leader election and when is it acceptable to enable it?:::Unclean leader election allows a broker not in the ISR (i.e., behind on replication) to be elected as the new partition leader when all ISR members are unavailable. This restores availability at the cost of data loss: any messages the old leader acknowledged but hadn't replicated to the new leader are permanently gone. It is acceptable in scenarios where availability is more important than durability — for example, click stream metrics, ad impressions, or IoT telemetry where occasional data loss is tolerable. It must never be enabled for financial transactions, order systems, or any data with strict durability requirements.

How does Kafka ensure the leader and followers stay in sync?:::Followers act as consumers of the leader's partition log, continuously sending FETCH requests. Each fetch specifies the offset the follower wants next; the leader returns available records. The leader tracks each follower's fetch position (Log End Offset) and the time of the last successful fetch. If a follower's LEO is equal to the leader's LEO, it is considered in-sync. The high watermark — the offset up to which all ISR members have replicated — advances as followers catch up, and consumers can only read up to the high watermark.
sessions.successCriteria: Can design a replication configuration for a given durability and availability target, explain ISR dynamics, and reason about the consequences of unclean leader election.

sessions.title: Exactly-Once Semantics — Idempotent Producer, Transactions, Read-Committed Consumer
objectives: Explain the three components of Kafka exactly-once semantics
Implement a transactional producer with initTransactions, beginTransaction, commitTransaction, and abortTransaction
Configure a consumer with isolation.level=read_committed
Explain the consume-transform-produce pattern and its role in end-to-end EOS
Describe the performance overhead of transactions and how to tune transaction.timeout.ms

activities.description: Implement a transactional producer that writes to two topics atomically. Simulate a failure mid-transaction (throw an exception before commitTransaction). Consume both topics with read_committed and verify neither topic received partial data.

Implement a consume-transform-produce pipeline: read from topic-A, double the numeric value, write to topic-B, atomically committing the consumer offset via sendOffsetsToTransaction. Restart the consumer mid-run and verify no duplicates in topic-B.

reviewQuestions: What is the difference between idempotent producer and transactional producer?:::An idempotent producer (enable.idempotence=true) prevents duplicate messages within a single producer session on a single partition by using Producer ID + sequence numbers. It is within-session only — a new process gets a new PID. A transactional producer (transactional.id set) adds cross-session identity and cross-partition atomicity: multiple sends to multiple partitions and offset commits can be grouped into a single atomic transaction that either all commits or all aborts. Idempotence is a prerequisite for transactions — enable.idempotence=true is automatically set when transactional.id is configured.

What does isolation.level=read_committed prevent the consumer from seeing?:::With read_committed, the consumer will not see: (1) records that are part of an open (in-progress) transaction, (2) records from aborted transactions. The consumer's read position is bounded by the Last Stable Offset (LSO) — it cannot advance past any open transaction. This ensures the consumer only processes records that are durably committed. Without this setting (default read_uncommitted), consumers see all records including those from open transactions that may later be aborted.

Why does Kafka EOS not cover external system writes?:::Kafka's transaction coordinator tracks offsets and records within Kafka's internal state. When a consumer reads a record and writes it to a database, Kafka can guarantee the Kafka-side commit is atomic, but the database write is outside Kafka's transaction scope. If the consumer crashes after the database write but before commitTransaction, Kafka will reprocess the record (replay from last committed offset) and the application will attempt to write to the database again. To handle this, the application must make the database write idempotent (e.g., upsert with the Kafka offset as primary key) or use the outbox pattern.

What happens if a transactional producer crashes mid-transaction and a new instance starts with the same transactional.id?:::The broker detects the new producer instance has the same transactional.id and bumps the producer epoch. The old instance (if still running) is fenced — any subsequent sends from it return ProducerFencedException. The new instance calls initTransactions(), which causes the broker to look up the incomplete transaction left by the old instance and abort it, cleaning up any uncommitted records. The new instance can then begin a fresh transaction. This ensures only one producer instance with a given transactional.id is active at a time.
sessions.successCriteria: Can implement a full exactly-once consume-transform-produce pipeline in Java, explain all three EOS components, and articulate why Kafka EOS does not extend to external systems.

sessions.title: Kafka Streams
objectives: Distinguish KStream from KTable and know when to use each
Write stateless and stateful stream processing pipelines using the Streams DSL
Configure tumbling, hopping, and session windows
Perform KStream-KTable joins and understand co-partitioning requirements

activities.description: Build a word-count Kafka Streams application locally using the DSL and verify output with kafka-console-consumer
Implement a KStream-KTable join that enriches clickstream events with user profile data from a compacted topic
Add a 5-minute tumbling window to count page views per user and write windowed results to an output topic

reviewQuestions: What is the difference between KStream and KTable?:::A KStream represents an unbounded, append-only event log — every record is an independent event. A KTable represents the latest value per key, functioning like a changelog-backed database table. KStreams are used for event sequences (clicks, transactions) while KTables model current state (user profiles, inventory). Under the hood, a KTable is backed by a Kafka topic and a local RocksDB state store.

What is co-partitioning and why is it required for KStream-KTable joins?:::Co-partitioning means both topics must have the same number of partitions and the same partitioning strategy (same key and partitioner). Kafka Streams assigns each task to exactly one partition per input topic; if partitions don't align, a task processing partition 3 of the stream might not have the corresponding KTable data for the same keys. Violations cause a TopologyException at startup.

How does Kafka Streams achieve fault tolerance for stateful operations?:::Each stateful processor (e.g., count, aggregate) writes state changes to a Kafka changelog topic backed by the same Kafka cluster. Locally the state is stored in RocksDB. On failure, a new instance restores state by replaying the changelog topic, so no state is lost.

Explain the difference between tumbling and hopping windows.:::A tumbling window has a fixed size and does not overlap — every event belongs to exactly one window (e.g., 1-minute buckets: [0–60s], [60–120s]). A hopping window has a fixed size but advances by a smaller step, causing overlap — an event can belong to multiple windows (e.g., 1-minute window every 30 seconds: [0–60s] and [30–90s] both include events at t=45s). Tumbling windows are simpler and suited for periodic reports; hopping windows suit sliding-average use cases.
sessions.successCriteria: Can build a complete Kafka Streams pipeline with at least one stateful operation, explain the KStream/KTable distinction under interview pressure, and configure windowed aggregations correctly.

sessions.title: Kafka Connect & Schema Registry
objectives: Understand Connect's source/sink architecture and deploy a connector via the REST API
Produce and consume Avro-encoded messages using Schema Registry
Explain schema evolution compatibility modes and choose the right one
Apply Single Message Transforms (SMTs) to modify records in-flight

activities.description: Deploy a Debezium MySQL CDC source connector using Docker Compose and verify change events flowing into Kafka topics
Write an Avro producer and consumer in Java using KafkaAvroSerializer; register a schema in Schema Registry and verify it via the REST API
Evolve the Avro schema by adding an optional field with a default value; confirm BACKWARD compatibility is not broken

reviewQuestions: What is Kafka Connect and why use it instead of a custom producer?:::Kafka Connect is a framework for reliably streaming data between Kafka and external systems. It provides built-in offset management, fault tolerance, REST-based configuration, and a rich ecosystem of pre-built connectors. A custom producer requires you to reimplement offset tracking, error handling, and restart logic. Connect offloads all of that, making it the standard choice for data ingestion and egress pipelines.

How does Schema Registry prevent data corruption in a Kafka pipeline?:::Schema Registry acts as a central schema store. Producers register their schema and receive a schema ID; the ID is embedded in each message (4 bytes after a magic byte). Consumers look up the schema by ID and deserialize accordingly. Crucially, the registry enforces a compatibility policy — if a producer tries to register a schema that breaks BACKWARD compatibility (e.g., removes a required field), the registration is rejected, preventing the message from ever reaching the topic.

What is BACKWARD schema compatibility and why is it the default?:::BACKWARD compatibility means a new schema version can read data written by the previous schema. This is the safest default because consumers are typically upgraded before producers. Adding a new optional field with a default value is BACKWARD compatible — older messages simply use the default for that field. Removing a required field is not, because old records have no value to fall back on.

What is CDC and how does Debezium use it?:::Change Data Capture (CDC) tracks row-level changes (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) in a database by reading the replication log (MySQL binlog, Postgres WAL). Debezium is a source connector that reads these logs and emits structured change events to Kafka topics — one topic per table. This approach captures every change with zero impact on the application and without polling.
sessions.successCriteria: Can explain the Connect architecture, deploy a connector via REST, produce Avro messages with Schema Registry, and reason about schema compatibility during an interview.

sessions.title: Performance Tuning
objectives: Explain the interaction between batch.size, linger.ms, and buffer.memory
Choose the right compression codec for a given throughput/latency tradeoff
Tune consumer fetch parameters to maximize poll throughput
Identify OS-level settings that impact Kafka broker performance

activities.description: Run kafka-producer-perf-test with default config, then with lz4 + batch.size=512KB + linger.ms=20 and compare MB/s throughput
Write a Java producer benchmark that measures records/sec under high-throughput vs low-latency configuration profiles
Use kafka-consumer-groups to observe consumer lag, then tune FETCH_MAX_BYTES and MAX_POLL_RECORDS until lag drops

reviewQuestions: How do batch.size and linger.ms interact?:::batch.size sets the maximum bytes in a single batch per partition; linger.ms is the maximum time the producer waits before sending an incomplete batch. A batch is sent as soon as either limit is reached. Setting linger.ms=0 sends records as fast as they arrive (low latency); setting linger.ms=20 lets the producer accumulate records for up to 20ms (higher throughput). batch.size is a hard upper bound — even with linger.ms=1000, if the batch fills up first, it sends immediately.

Which compression codec should you use for a high-throughput, CPU-sensitive pipeline?:::lz4 is the best choice: it has the lowest CPU overhead, decompresses extremely fast (useful on the consumer side), and provides a good 2–3x compression ratio. snappy is a close second. gzip has the best compression ratio but its CPU cost can become a bottleneck at very high throughput. zstd (available since Kafka 2.1) offers the best ratio-to-CPU balance for modern use cases.

What is buffer.memory and what happens when it fills up?:::buffer.memory is the total memory the producer allocates to buffer records waiting to be sent. When the buffer is full, send() blocks for max.block.ms (default 60 seconds) waiting for space. If the buffer does not free up in time, a TimeoutException is thrown. You should increase buffer.memory if your application produces faster than the broker can accept, or if linger.ms is high.

Why does vm.swappiness matter for Kafka brokers?:::Kafka's read performance heavily depends on the Linux page cache — recently written segments are served from RAM, not disk. If the OS swaps page cache pages to disk (even partially), read latency spikes dramatically. Setting vm.swappiness=1 tells the kernel to avoid swapping almost entirely, preferring to drop file cache pages only as a last resort, which keeps Kafka's hot data in RAM.
sessions.successCriteria: Can walk through the producer tuning parameters in an interview, justify codec choice with trade-offs, and demonstrate familiarity with kafka-producer-perf-test and consumer lag monitoring.

sessions.title: Kafka Security
sessions.content:
## Kafka Security

```mermaid
graph TB
    P[Producer] --> T[Topic]
    T --> P0[Partition 0]
    T --> P1[Partition 1]
    T --> P2[Partition 2]
    P0 --> CG1[Consumer Group 1]
    P1 --> CG1
    P2 --> CG2[Consumer Group 2]
```


A default Kafka installation has no authentication or encryption. Production deployments must configure SSL/TLS for encryption in transit, SASL for authentication, and ACLs for authorization.

### Security Layers

```
1. Encryption in transit — SSL/TLS
2. Authentication        — SASL (PLAIN, SCRAM, GSSAPI/Kerberos, OAUTHBEARER)
3. Authorization         — ACLs (Access Control Lists)
4. Encryption at rest    — Disk encryption (OS-level, not Kafka native)
```

### SSL/TLS Configuration

**Broker (`server.properties`):**

```properties
listeners=SSL://broker:9093
ssl.keystore.location=/var/ssl/kafka.broker.keystore.jks
ssl.keystore.password=changeit
ssl.key.password=changeit
ssl.truststore.location=/var/ssl/kafka.broker.truststore.jks
ssl.truststore.password=changeit
ssl.client.auth=required
ssl.endpoint.identification.algorithm=https
```

**Java client configuration:**

```java
import org.apache.kafka.clients.producer.*;
import org.apache.kafka.common.config.SslConfigs;
import java.util.Properties;

public class SecureProducer {

    public static Properties sslConfig() {
        Properties props = new Properties();
        props.put(ProducerConfig.BOOTSTRAP_SERVERS_CONFIG, "broker:9093");
        props.put(ProducerConfig.KEY_SERIALIZER_CLASS_CONFIG,
            "org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.StringSerializer");
        props.put(ProducerConfig.VALUE_SERIALIZER_CLASS_CONFIG,
            "org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.StringSerializer");

        // SSL encryption
        props.put("security.protocol", "SSL");
        props.put(SslConfigs.SSL_TRUSTSTORE_LOCATION_CONFIG,
            "/var/ssl/client.truststore.jks");
        props.put(SslConfigs.SSL_TRUSTSTORE_PASSWORD_CONFIG, "changeit");
        props.put(SslConfigs.SSL_KEYSTORE_LOCATION_CONFIG,
            "/var/ssl/client.keystore.jks");
        props.put(SslConfigs.SSL_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD_CONFIG, "changeit");
        props.put(SslConfigs.SSL_KEY_PASSWORD_CONFIG, "changeit");
        props.put(SslConfigs.SSL_ENDPOINT_IDENTIFICATION_ALGORITHM_CONFIG, "https");

        return props;
    }

    public static Properties saslSslConfig(String username, String password) {
        Properties props = new Properties();
        props.put(ProducerConfig.BOOTSTRAP_SERVERS_CONFIG, "broker:9094");
        props.put(ProducerConfig.KEY_SERIALIZER_CLASS_CONFIG,
            "org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.StringSerializer");
        props.put(ProducerConfig.VALUE_SERIALIZER_CLASS_CONFIG,
            "org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.StringSerializer");

        // SASL_SSL = SASL authentication + SSL encryption
        props.put("security.protocol", "SASL_SSL");
        props.put("sasl.mechanism", "SCRAM-SHA-512");
        props.put("sasl.jaas.config",
            "org.apache.kafka.common.security.scram.ScramLoginModule required "
            + "username=\"" + username + "\" "
            + "password=\"" + password + "\";");
        props.put(SslConfigs.SSL_TRUSTSTORE_LOCATION_CONFIG,
            "/var/ssl/client.truststore.jks");
        props.put(SslConfigs.SSL_TRUSTSTORE_PASSWORD_CONFIG, "changeit");

        return props;
    }
}
```

### ACLs — Authorization

ACLs define which principals (users) can perform which operations on which resources.

```bash
# Grant producer permissions to service account 'order-service'
kafka-acls.sh --bootstrap-server broker:9094 \
  --command-config /etc/kafka/admin.properties \
  --add \
  --allow-principal User:order-service \
  --operation Write \
  --operation Describe \
  --topic orders

# Grant consumer permissions to 'analytics-service'
kafka-acls.sh --bootstrap-server broker:9094 \
  --command-config /etc/kafka/admin.properties \
  --add \
  --allow-principal User:analytics-service \
  --operation Read \
  --operation Describe \
  --topic orders \
  --group analytics-cg

# List all ACLs
kafka-acls.sh --bootstrap-server broker:9094 \
  --command-config /etc/kafka/admin.properties \
  --list
```

### ACL Resource Types

| Resource | Example |
|----------|---------|
| Topic | `--topic orders` |
| Consumer Group | `--group my-cg` |
| Cluster | `--cluster` (admin ops) |
| TransactionalId | `--transactional-id tx-1` |

### SASL Mechanisms Comparison

| Mechanism | Use Case | Security |
|-----------|----------|----------|
| PLAIN | Dev/internal only | Password in clear (needs SSL) |
| SCRAM-SHA-256/512 | Most production clusters | Challenge-response, no plaintext |
| GSSAPI (Kerberos) | Enterprise / on-prem | Strongest, complex setup |
| OAUTHBEARER | Cloud-native / OIDC | Token-based, integrates with IdP |

### Interview Tips
- `SASL_SSL` is the recommended protocol: SASL provides authentication, SSL provides encryption.
- `ssl.endpoint.identification.algorithm=https` enables hostname verification — never disable this in production.
- `super.users` in `server.properties` bypasses ACLs — restrict to ops accounts only.
- Kafka does not support row-level security; ACLs operate at the topic level.
objectives: Configure SSL/TLS on brokers and Java clients
Set up SASL/SCRAM authentication for producers and consumers
Create and manage ACLs to enforce least-privilege access
Compare SASL mechanisms and choose the right one for a given environment

activities.description: Generate a self-signed CA, create broker and client keystores/truststores, and configure a local Kafka broker for SSL-only communication
Enable SASL/SCRAM on the broker, create a test user with kafka-configs, and update your Java producer to use SASL_SSL
Write ACL rules granting a producer-only user write access to a topic and verify that read attempts from the same user are denied

reviewQuestions: What is the difference between SSL and SASL_SSL in Kafka?:::SSL configures only encryption in transit — the connection is encrypted but the broker does not authenticate the client's identity beyond checking the client certificate. SASL_SSL combines SASL (authentication mechanism like SCRAM or Kerberos) with SSL (encryption). SASL_SSL is the recommended setting for production: you get a verified identity (SASL) and an encrypted channel (SSL). Plain SSL with mutual TLS also achieves authentication via client certificates, but SASL_SSL is more flexible for username/password or token-based auth.

What does an ACL in Kafka consist of?:::A Kafka ACL has four components: (1) Principal — the authenticated identity, e.g., User:order-service; (2) Resource — the type and name, e.g., Topic:orders; (3) Operation — the action, e.g., Read, Write, Describe, Create, Delete; (4) Permission — Allow or Deny. ACLs are stored in ZooKeeper (or KRaft's metadata log) and enforced by the broker's Authorizer plugin (default: AclAuthorizer).

Why should you always enable ssl.endpoint.identification.algorithm=https?:::Hostname verification checks that the CN or SAN in the broker's certificate matches the hostname the client is connecting to. Without it, a man-in-the-middle attacker could present any valid certificate signed by your CA and intercept traffic. Setting it to 'https' enables hostname verification using the same algorithm as HTTPS, closing this attack vector.

What SASL mechanism would you recommend for a cloud-native Kafka cluster integrated with an identity provider?:::OAUTHBEARER is the best fit for cloud-native environments. It integrates with standard OAuth 2.0 / OIDC identity providers (Okta, Keycloak, Auth0), supports short-lived tokens that rotate automatically, and avoids managing long-lived passwords. The broker validates the token's signature and expiry without calling the IdP on every request. SCRAM is simpler and fine for clusters without an existing IdP.
sessions.successCriteria: Can explain the three-layer security model (encryption, authentication, authorization), configure a Java client for SASL_SSL, and write correct kafka-acls.sh commands during a system design or security discussion.

sessions.title: Kafka in Microservices
objectives: Implement Event Sourcing using Kafka as the event store and rebuild state by replay
Explain choreography vs orchestration Sagas and their trade-offs
Implement the Outbox pattern to guarantee transactional event publishing
Route unprocessable messages to a dead letter topic with error metadata

activities.description: Design a multi-service order flow using Saga choreography: draw the event flow diagram with happy path and compensation events
Implement the Outbox pattern in Java: write a service that inserts to an orders table and outbox table in a single transaction, then write a relay that reads the outbox and publishes to Kafka
Add a dead letter topic handler to an existing consumer that retries 3 times then routes failures to orders.DLT with error headers

reviewQuestions: What problem does the Outbox pattern solve?:::Without the Outbox pattern, publishing to Kafka and persisting to a database are two independent operations. If the service crashes after the DB commit but before the Kafka send, the event is lost. If it crashes after Kafka but before the DB commit, the event is published but the DB is inconsistent. The Outbox pattern solves this by writing the event to an outbox table in the same database transaction as the business data. A separate relay process publishes from the outbox to Kafka, achieving guaranteed at-least-once delivery.

What is the difference between Saga choreography and orchestration?:::In choreography, each service reacts to domain events and emits its own events — there is no central coordinator. This is highly decoupled but can be hard to trace and reason about. In orchestration, a central Saga orchestrator sends commands to each service and waits for replies. This makes the business flow explicit and easier to observe but introduces a coordinator that becomes a coupling point. Choreography suits simple flows; orchestration suits complex multi-step transactions.

How do you prevent duplicate processing in an event-driven microservice?:::Implement idempotent consumers: assign each event a unique ID (e.g., orderId + eventType + timestamp), and before processing, check a deduplication store (Redis, DB) whether the event ID has already been processed. If it has, skip it. Only store the ID after successful processing. This pattern is essential in Saga compensations where at-least-once delivery can cause the same compensating event to arrive multiple times.

What is a Dead Letter Topic and when should you use it?:::A Dead Letter Topic (DLT) receives messages that a consumer cannot process after exhausting retries. Instead of blocking the partition forever or silently dropping the message, the consumer publishes the message to a DLT (typically named originalTopic.DLT) with error metadata headers. This unblocks partition processing while preserving the problematic message for inspection and replay after the bug is fixed. DLTs should be monitored with alerts — a growing DLT indicates a systemic bug.
sessions.successCriteria: Can design a complete event-driven order processing system using the Outbox pattern and Saga choreography on a whiteboard, explain the trade-offs vs REST-based orchestration, and implement dead letter topic handling.

sessions.title: Production Operations
sessions.content:
## Kafka Production Operations

```mermaid
graph TB
    P[Producer] --> T[Topic]
    T --> P0[Partition 0]
    T --> P1[Partition 1]
    T --> P2[Partition 2]
    P0 --> CG1[Consumer Group 1]
    P1 --> CG1
    P2 --> CG2[Consumer Group 2]
```


Running Kafka in production requires careful upfront planning (cluster sizing, partition count) and ongoing operations (monitoring, upgrades, disaster recovery). This session covers the practitioner knowledge that separates engineers who have 'used Kafka' from those who have 'operated Kafka.'

### Cluster Sizing

**Step 1 — Calculate storage:**

```
Storage = message_rate (msg/s)
        × avg_message_size (bytes)
        × retention_period (seconds)
        × replication_factor

Example:
  10,000 msg/s × 1 KB × 604,800 s (7 days) × 3 replicas
  = 18.1 TB raw storage
  → 3 brokers × 6 TB each (with 20% headroom)
```

**Step 2 — Network capacity:**

```
Ingress = message_rate × avg_size = 10,000 × 1 KB = 10 MB/s
Egress  = ingress × consumer_groups = 10 MB/s × 5 groups = 50 MB/s total
Broker NIC requirement = egress / num_brokers = 50 MB/s / 3 ≈ 17 MB/s each
```

**Minimum production cluster:** 3 brokers (for RF=3), 3 ZooKeeper nodes (or KRaft mode for Kafka 3.3+).

### Partition Count Planning

```java
import org.apache.kafka.clients.admin.*;
import java.util.*;

public class TopicAdminExample {

    public static void createTopic(String bootstrapServers,
                                   String topicName,
                                   int partitions,
                                   short replicationFactor) throws Exception {
        Properties props = new Properties();
        props.put(AdminClientConfig.BOOTSTRAP_SERVERS_CONFIG, bootstrapServers);

        try (AdminClient admin = AdminClient.create(props)) {
            NewTopic topic = new NewTopic(topicName, partitions, replicationFactor);
            topic.configs(Map.of(
                "retention.ms", String.valueOf(7 * 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000L), // 7 days
                "compression.type", "lz4",
                "min.insync.replicas", "2"
            ));

            admin.createTopics(List.of(topic)).all().get();
            System.out.printf("Created topic %s with %d partitions%n",
                topicName, partitions);
        }
    }

    // Rule of thumb: partitions = max(target_throughput / single_partition_throughput, consumers)
    public static int recommendedPartitions(long targetMBps,
                                            long partitionMBps,
                                            int maxConsumers) {
        long byThroughput = (long) Math.ceil((double) targetMBps / partitionMBps);
        return (int) Math.max(byThroughput, maxConsumers);
    }
}
```

**Partition guidelines:**
- A single partition achieves ~10–100 MB/s (disk bound).
- Max consumers in a group = number of partitions (extras sit idle).
- Over-partitioning wastes file handles and memory; under-partitioning limits throughput.
- Common starting points: 12, 24, or 48 partitions (divisible by common consumer counts).

### Monitoring — Key Metrics

| Metric | Source | Alert Threshold |
|--------|--------|-----------------|
| `kafka.consumer.consumer-fetch-manager-metrics.records-lag-max` | JMX | > 10,000 |
| `kafka.server.BrokerTopicMetrics.BytesInPerSec` | JMX | > 80% NIC capacity |
| `kafka.server.ReplicaManager.UnderReplicatedPartitions` | JMX | > 0 |
| `kafka.controller.KafkaController.ActiveControllerCount` | JMX | != 1 |
| `kafka.network.SocketServer.RequestHandlerAvgIdlePercent` | JMX | < 30% |

```bash
# Check consumer lag from CLI
kafka-consumer-groups.sh \
  --bootstrap-server broker:9092 \
  --describe \
  --group my-consumer-group

# Output columns:
# TOPIC  PARTITION  CURRENT-OFFSET  LOG-END-OFFSET  LAG  CONSUMER-ID
```

### Rolling Upgrades

```
Proper upgrade sequence (zero downtime):

1. Check inter.broker.protocol.version and log.message.format.version
2. Upgrade one broker at a time:
   a. Graceful shutdown: kafka-server-stop.sh
   b. Wait for partitions to reassign (UnderReplicatedPartitions → 0)
   c. Install new Kafka version
   d. Restart broker
3. After all brokers are on new version:
   a. Update inter.broker.protocol.version to new version
   b. Update log.message.format.version to new version
4. Verify with kafka-broker-api-versions.sh
```

### Disaster Recovery

**Active-Passive with MirrorMaker 2:**

```properties
# MirrorMaker 2 configuration
clusters = primary, secondary
primary.bootstrap.servers = primary-kafka:9092
secondary.bootstrap.servers = secondary-kafka:9092

primary->secondary.enabled = true
primary->secondary.topics = .*
primary->secondary.replication.factor = 3

# Consumer offset sync — enables near-zero RPO failover
offset-syncs.topic.replication.factor = 3
checkpoints.topic.replication.factor = 3
sync.group.offsets.enabled = true
```

**RPO vs RTO:**
- **RPO** (Recovery Point Objective): How much data can you afford to lose? MirrorMaker 2 with `sync.group.offsets.enabled=true` achieves near-zero RPO.
- **RTO** (Recovery Time Objective): How quickly must the system recover? Pre-warm consumers on secondary cluster to reduce RTO to minutes.

### Interview Tips
- UnderReplicatedPartitions > 0 is a critical alert — it means data durability is compromised.
- Never decrease partition count (not supported). Always add partitions, never remove.
- `min.insync.replicas=2` with `replication.factor=3` and `acks=all` is the gold standard for durability.
- Prefer KRaft mode for new clusters (Kafka 3.3+) — eliminates ZooKeeper dependency.
objectives: Size a Kafka cluster given throughput, retention, and replication requirements
Choose the right partition count using the throughput-per-partition formula
Monitor cluster health using JMX metrics and consumer lag
Execute a rolling upgrade without downtime and configure MirrorMaker 2 for DR

activities.description: Given a scenario (50,000 msg/s, 2 KB avg, 14-day retention, RF=3), calculate required storage, NIC capacity, and recommended broker count
Set up Prometheus + Grafana with the Kafka JMX Exporter and create a dashboard that shows consumer lag, under-replicated partitions, and bytes-in-per-sec
Simulate a broker failure in a local 3-broker Docker cluster, observe partition leadership changes, and verify that a consumer group continues processing with lag

reviewQuestions: How do you decide the number of partitions for a new topic?:::Use the formula: partitions = max(target_throughput_MBps / single_partition_throughput_MBps, max_consumers_in_group). A single partition typically handles 10–100 MB/s depending on hardware. If your target is 500 MB/s and a partition does 50 MB/s, you need at least 10 partitions. Also ensure partitions >= max consumers so every consumer gets at least one. Choose a number divisible by common consumer counts (e.g., 12, 24, 48). Err on the side of more partitions — you cannot reduce them without recreating the topic.

What does UnderReplicatedPartitions > 0 mean and why is it critical?:::UnderReplicatedPartitions means at least one partition has fewer in-sync replicas (ISRs) than the configured replication factor. This indicates a broker is down, lagging, or experiencing I/O issues. It is critical because if another broker fails while partitions are under-replicated, you can lose data. With acks=all and min.insync.replicas=2, producers will also start receiving exceptions. Alert on this metric immediately and investigate the lagging broker.

What is the correct procedure for a zero-downtime Kafka broker upgrade?:::Upgrade one broker at a time: (1) gracefully stop the broker (kafka-server-stop.sh), which triggers partition leadership to transfer to other brokers; (2) wait until UnderReplicatedPartitions returns to 0, confirming all partitions are fully replicated; (3) install the new version and restart; (4) confirm the broker rejoins the cluster and catches up. After all brokers are upgraded, update inter.broker.protocol.version and log.message.format.version to the new version. Never upgrade all brokers simultaneously.

How does MirrorMaker 2 support disaster recovery?:::MirrorMaker 2 replicates topics and consumer group offsets from a primary Kafka cluster to a secondary cluster. With sync.group.offsets.enabled=true, consumer offsets are translated and synced to the secondary, so consumers can resume from the correct position after a failover. This achieves near-zero RPO (minimal data loss). For RTO, pre-warming consumer groups on the secondary cluster means failover can complete in minutes. Topic names on the secondary are prefixed with the source cluster alias (e.g., primary.orders).
sessions.successCriteria: Can size a Kafka cluster from scratch given requirements, explain the top 5 JMX metrics to monitor, describe the rolling upgrade procedure, and design a DR setup using MirrorMaker 2 in a system design interview.

quizBank.question: What is the maximum number of consumers in a consumer group that can actively consume from a topic with 6 partitions?
quizBank.explanation: Each partition is assigned to exactly one consumer within a group. With 6 partitions, at most 6 consumers can be active simultaneously. A 7th consumer would be idle, waiting in case a current member leaves. This is why partition count determines maximum parallelism — plan partition count based on your peak consumer count target.

quizBank.question: A producer sends a message with acks=all to a topic with replication.factor=3 and min.insync.replicas=2. Two brokers fail simultaneously. What happens?
options: A) Message is written to the surviving broker and acknowledged
B) Producer receives NotEnoughReplicasException and the write is rejected
C) Message is queued locally and retried automatically
D) Kafka elects an out-of-sync replica as leader and writes succeed
quizBank.explanation: With min.insync.replicas=2 and only 1 broker surviving, the ISR size (1) is below the minimum threshold. Kafka rejects the write with NotEnoughReplicasException rather than acknowledge a message that cannot meet the durability guarantee. This is the correct production behavior — a silent acknowledgment on 1 replica with RF=3 would risk data loss. The producer should be configured with retries to handle transient broker failures.

quizBank.question: Which Kafka configuration ensures a consumer reads only messages from committed transactions?
quizBank.explanation: isolation.level=read_committed causes the consumer to skip any messages that are part of an open or aborted transaction. Only records from committed transactions are returned by poll(). The default is read_uncommitted which returns all records including those from in-flight transactions. This setting is essential when consuming from topics written by transactional producers to achieve end-to-end exactly-once semantics.

quizBank.question: Explain what the ISR (In-Sync Replicas) set represents and why it matters for durability.
quizBank.correctAnswer: The ISR is the set of partition replicas that are fully caught up with the leader, within the window defined by replica.lag.time.max.ms. It matters because: (1) acks=all waits for all ISR members to acknowledge the write, so the ISR size determines how many brokers must confirm; (2) only ISR members are eligible for clean leader election, preventing data loss when the current leader fails; (3) if the ISR shrinks below min.insync.replicas, producers with acks=all will be rejected, making ISR size a live durability signal.
quizBank.explanation: The ISR is the set of partition replicas that are fully caught up with the leader, within the window defined by replica.lag.time.max.ms. It matters because: (1) acks=all waits for all ISR members to acknowledge the write, so the ISR size determines how many brokers must confirm; (2) only ISR members are eligible for clean leader election, preventing data loss when the current leader fails; (3) if the ISR shrinks below min.insync.replicas, producers with acks=all will be rejected, making ISR size a live durability signal.

quizBank.question: What happens to ordering guarantees when a Kafka producer has max.in.flight.requests.per.connection=5 and retries > 0 but enable.idempotence=false?
options: A) Ordering is fully preserved because Kafka re-orders on the broker side
B) Ordering can be violated if a retry of an earlier batch succeeds after a later batch
C) Ordering is only affected across partitions, never within a single partition
D) Retries are disabled when max.in.flight > 1
quizBank.explanation: Without idempotence, if batch N fails and is retried while batch N+1 has already been written, batch N's retry can land after N+1, violating partition-level ordering. The fix is either: (a) set enable.idempotence=true which uses producer sequence numbers to enforce ordering even with 5 in-flight requests, or (b) set max.in.flight.requests.per.connection=1 (no parallelism, correct ordering but lower throughput). Idempotence is the recommended approach and is now enabled by default in Kafka 3.0+.

quizBank.question: What is consumer group rebalancing and name two events that trigger it?
quizBank.correctAnswer: Rebalancing is the process of redistributing topic partitions among the active members of a consumer group. It is triggered by: (1) a new consumer joining the group, (2) an existing consumer leaving (graceful shutdown or heartbeat timeout), (3) the partition count of a subscribed topic changing, (4) a consumer failing to call poll() within max.poll.interval.ms. During rebalancing, all consumption pauses until the new assignment is complete.
quizBank.explanation: Rebalancing is the process of redistributing topic partitions among the active members of a consumer group. It is triggered by: (1) a new consumer joining the group, (2) an existing consumer leaving (graceful shutdown or heartbeat timeout), (3) the partition count of a subscribed topic changing, (4) a consumer failing to call poll() within max.poll.interval.ms. During rebalancing, all consumption pauses until the new assignment is complete.

quizBank.question: Which producer configuration is required when enable.idempotence=true?
quizBank.explanation: Idempotent producers require acks=all because the broker needs acknowledgment from all in-sync replicas to safely assign and track producer sequence numbers. With acks=0 or acks=1, the broker cannot guarantee the sequence number was durably stored before a crash, breaking the deduplication guarantee. Kafka automatically sets acks=all, retries=Integer.MAX_VALUE, and max.in.flight.requests.per.connection=5 when you enable idempotence.

quizBank.question: A topic has replication.factor=3. The leader broker for a partition crashes. Under what condition can Kafka elect a new leader without data loss?
quizBank.correctAnswer: Kafka can elect a new leader without data loss only if at least one member of the ISR (In-Sync Replicas set) is still alive. ISR members are fully caught up with the leader, so electing any of them preserves all committed messages. If unclean.leader.election.enable=false (recommended for production), Kafka waits for an ISR member to become available rather than elect an out-of-sync replica. If unclean.leader.election.enable=true, Kafka may elect an out-of-sync follower, restoring availability at the cost of potential data loss (messages acknowledged by the old leader but not yet replicated to the new one).
quizBank.explanation: Kafka can elect a new leader without data loss only if at least one member of the ISR (In-Sync Replicas set) is still alive. ISR members are fully caught up with the leader, so electing any of them preserves all committed messages. If unclean.leader.election.enable=false (recommended for production), Kafka waits for an ISR member to become available rather than elect an out-of-sync replica. If unclean.leader.election.enable=true, Kafka may elect an out-of-sync follower, restoring availability at the cost of potential data loss.

quizBank.question: What is the role of the __consumer_offsets topic in Kafka?
options: A) Stores schema definitions for Avro messages
B) Stores committed consumer offsets per group per partition
C) Stores broker metadata and controller election state
quizBank.explanation: __consumer_offsets is an internal Kafka topic with 50 partitions (by default) that stores the committed offset for each (consumer group, topic, partition) triplet. When a consumer commits offsets (manually or via auto-commit), Kafka writes a record to this topic. On restart or rebalance, consumers fetch their last committed offset from this topic to resume from where they left off. It is compacted so only the latest offset per key is retained.

quizBank.question: Describe the difference between at-least-once, at-most-once, and exactly-once delivery semantics in Kafka.
quizBank.correctAnswer: At-most-once: offsets are committed before processing. If the consumer crashes after commit but before processing, those messages are skipped — data loss is possible but no duplicates. At-least-once: offsets are committed after successful processing. If the consumer crashes after processing but before committing, messages are reprocessed on restart — duplicates possible but no data loss. Exactly-once: achieved in Kafka via idempotent producers (dedup on write side) + transactional offset commits (atomic write + offset) + read_committed isolation on consumer. This ensures each message's effect is applied exactly once in the system, requiring all three components working together.
quizBank.explanation: At-most-once: commit before processing — possible data loss, no duplicates. At-least-once: commit after processing — no data loss, possible duplicates. Exactly-once: idempotent producer + transactional offset commits + read_committed consumer — no loss, no duplicates. Each stronger guarantee trades off latency and throughput.
